rulings.Case Title: SPIRO ZOGRAFOS VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, AUGUST 20, 2007, LINE 14. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiff's theory of liability, as well as that of co-defendant Morey, is that the City is liable under Govt. Code § 815.6 for failing to discharge its mandatory duty to provide a safe place to work (Labor Code §§ 6400 et seq.) and/or for violations of public policy for either failing to protect plaintiff from workplace violence after his OHSA complaints or in wrongfully discharging him as a consequence of his complaints. The City's liability is limited by Govt. Code § 815 to liability imposed upon the City by statute as well as immunities granted to public employees. The decision in Ross v. San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District (2007) 146 Cal.App.4th 1507 makes clear that the City has neither direct nor vicarious liability for the common law torts of discrimination or wrongful termination of its employees. To the extent plaintiff's claim is based on such conduct, it fails as a matter of law. Plaintiff's reliance on Govt. Code § 815.6 and the Labor Code is misplaced. The Labor Code provisions do not impose a mandatory duty on the City; rather, they impose duties on all employers. Bradford v. State of California (1973) 36 Cal.App.3d 16 illustrates the point for the statute in Bradford applied expressly to the State's obligation to process criminal records; it was duty imposed by statute on a public entity. To the extent that plaintiff claims the Labor Code provisions establish a duty of care that the City and/or its employees failed to meet, the rationale of Ross is applicable and under Govt. Code §§ 815 and 820.2, the City would have no liability. =(302/PJM/AP)

COMMUNITIES, AND LYTTON GARDENS SENIOR COMMUNITIES' MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. =(302/PJM/PB)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES R BANCROFT VS. EPISCOPAL HOMES FOUNDATION,A TAX-EXEMPT CALIFORNIA
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, AUGUST 20, 2007, LINE 12. DEFENDANT EPISCOPAL HOMES FOUNDATION, EHF FUND, INC., EPISCOPAL SENIOR COMMUNITIES, EPISCOPAL COMMUNITIES FOUNDATION, JOHN TENANT MEMORIAL HOMES, J.T.M. COMMUNITIES AND LYTTON GARDENS SENIOR COMMUNITIES' DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT: Hearing required to determine whether there is a basis to amend. The demurrer is sustained. On its face, the Life Care Contract contains express provisions that appear to preclude recovery by plaintiff on the basic theory of the case, i.e. that the Episcopal Diocese and Church were obligated to exercise continuing oversight over plaintiff's residence and/or the Foundation that owned and/or operated the facility. Specifically, plaintiff's contract is only with the Foundation. The contract recitals declare that the Church and its subdivisions do not accept "any financial, contractual or other legal responsibility for the Foundation, its contracts, acts or other duties or liabilities." Further, paragraph 11 states that the rights of plaintiff are limited by the contract and the contract expressly precludes management rights. =(302/PJM/PB)
